Abstract
--------

### Background and Aim

The traditional drug delivery approach involves systemic administration of a drug that could be nonspecific in targeting, low on efficacy, and with severe side‐effects. To address such challenges, the field of smart drug delivery has emerged aiming at designing and developing delivery systems that can target specific cells, tissues, and organs and have minimal off‐target side‐effects.

### Methods

A literature search was done to collate papers and reports about the currently available various strategies for smart nano‐inspired drug delivery. The databases searched were PubMed, Scopus, and Google Scholar. Based on selection criteria, the most pertinent and recent items were included.

### Results

Smart drug delivery is a cutting‐edge revolutionary intervention in modern medicines to ensure effective and safe administration of therapeutics to target sites. These hold great promise for targeted and controlled delivery of therapeutic agents to improve the efficacy with reduced side‐effects as compared to the conventional drug delivery approaches. Current smart drug delivery approaches include nanoparticles, liposomes, micelles, and hydrogels, each with its own advantages and limitations. The success of these delivery systems lies in engineering and designing them, and optimizing their pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics properties.

### Conclusion

Development of drug delivery systems that can get beyond various physiological and clinical barriers, as observed in conventionally administered chemotherapeutics, has been possible through recent advancements. Using multifunctional targeting methodologies, smart drug delivery tries to localize therapy to the target location, reduces cytotoxicity, and improves the therapeutic index. Rapid advancements in research and development in smart drug delivery provide wider and more promising avenues to guarantee a better healthcare system, improve patient outcomes, and achieve higher levels of effective medical interventions like personalized medicine.

**Keywords:** hydrogels, liposomes, micelles, nanoparticles, smart drug delivery, therapeutics

1. INTRODUCTION
---------------

Drug delivery is a critical aspect of modern medicine that ensures effective and safe administration of therapeutic agents to the target site in the body.[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0001), [2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0002) Traditional drug delivery or systemic administration of a drug could have flaws such as nonspecific targeting, low efficacy, and inadvertent and obvious side‐effects.[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0003), [4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0004) The emerging field of smart drug delivery aims to design and develop delivery systems to address these flaws to target specific cells, tissues, and organs better and minimize the off‐target side‐effects.[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0005), [6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0006)

The development of smart drug delivery systems has opened up new vistas for targeted drug delivery against numerous diseases including cancer, cardiovascular diseases, neurological disorders, and infectious diseases. Through precise delivery of therapeutic agents, smart drug delivery systems could potentially revolutionize the field of medicines in particular and healthcare in general.[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0007) Smart drug delivery systems adopt various approaches for improved specificity, efficacy, and safety of drug delivery. These include using nanoparticles (NPs), liposomes, micelles, hydrogels, and other such as biomaterials that can improve drug solubility, stability, and bioavailability. Also, designed smart drug delivery systems could respond to external or internal stimuli, and allow for controlled drug release at the target site.[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0008)

This review attempts to provide an overview of the current approaches and latest trends in smart drug delivery, various delivery systems, and their applications. The advantages and limitations of each approach are discussed, and the recent advances and challenges in the field are highlighted. The discussion extends to the future directions and potential applications of smart drug delivery systems beyond conventional therapeutics to personalized medicine and combination therapy. Supporting substrates, flexible circuitry, adhesive films, actuator components, thin‐film sensor systems, data transmission systems, therapeutic systems, and energy‐harvesting systems are typically included in smart wearable patch systems. By tracking numerous physical and biochemical characteristics on real‐time basis and displaying an on‐demand closed‐loop drug release, smart wearable patch systems that are coupled with therapeutic components can enable personal healthcare and medication. Because they combine biosensors and stimuli‐responsive carrier‐based DDSs, these user‐friendly, long‐acting, noninvasive and self‐administrable smart wearable patch systems have the potential to usher in an array of new‐age healthcare platforms.[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0009)

2. METHODOLOGY
--------------

To find the currently available papers and studies on the various strategies for smart nano‐inspired drug delivery, a focused literature search was carrier out. The most pertinent and recent literature, as per the selection criteria, was concentrated on. The keywords and their combinations used while carrying out the targeted online search were “smart nano drug delivery,” “smart nano drug delivery system,” “nanostructured smart drug delivery,” “precise nano drug delivery,” and “personalized nano drug delivery.” The scientific databases searched were PubMed, Scopus, and Google Scholar. The requirements for an article to meet the inclusion criteria were: 1. studies or articles on smart nano drug delivery, 2. studies or articles published in the English language, 3. studies or articles published from the earliest possible date till June 2023, and 5. studies or publications with June 2023 or later as the publication time. A total of 69 articles were analyzed out of 240 collated after searching, and the references were validated and manually listed. Based on the above selection criteria, the most pertinent and recent items were included. The findings shed light on the progress in the field of smart nano‐drug delivery. Patterns and consistency throughout the included literature may be found due to the narrative synthesis technique employed in the study. The irrelevant or less meaningful articles were excluded to avoid defocusing from the write‐up. The potential publishing and linguistic bias, biasness due to high dependence on earlier reported research, the lack of generalizability, and the contradicting evidence are some of the limitations of this piece of work.

3. NP‐BASED SMART DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEM
--------------------------------------

NP‐based smart drug delivery system is one of the most extensively improvised and widely applied approaches for targeted drug delivery. NPs are widely defined as particles with a 1–1000 nm size range having the ability to penetrate deep into the cells and tissues, providing enhanced drug delivery efficiency.[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0007), [8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0008) They can also be designed with specific surface properties like charge, hydrophobicity, or functionality, to enhance cellular uptake, extend circulation time, and target the specific cells or tissues better.[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0010), [11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0011)

NPs can be made from a variety of materials like polymer, lipid, metal, and inorganic material, each with its own advantages and limitations (Figure[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-fig-0001)). For instance, polymer‐based NP poly (lactic‐co‐glycolic acid; PLGA) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) are suitable for in vivo applications owing to their biocompatibility and biodegradability. Metal‐based NPs like gold and iron oxide have unique optical and magnetic properties, useful for imaging and theranostic applications. Lipid‐based NPs like liposomes and solid lipid NPs can mimic the structure of a cell membrane, enabling better cellular uptake and targeting.[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0012), [13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0013) NPs can also be functionalized with targeting ligands like antibodies, peptides, or aptamers to selectively bind to specific cell surface receptors and enhance drug delivery specificity. Also, the designed stimuli‐responsive NPs release a drug in response to specific triggers such as an altered pH, temperature or enzyme activity, that ensures target‐specific controlled drug release.[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0007)

Classes of NPs for smart drug delivery and their advantages and disadvantages. NP, nanoparticle.

Several NP‐based smart drug delivery systems are developed against numerous diseases like cancer, cardiovascular diseases, and neurological disorders. For example, designed NP‐based drug delivery systems target cancer cells by exploiting the overexpression of specific receptors or enzymes like folate receptors or matrix metalloproteinases that are abundant in onco‐cells. Targeting strategies could improve the efficacy and reduce toxicity of chemotherapeutic agents like paclitaxel or doxorubicin.[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0014), [15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0015) Besides cancer, NP‐based drug delivery systems are developed against cardiovascular and neurological ailments. Developed NP‐based systems deliver anti‐inflammatory agents like siRNA or curcumin to the vascular inflammation site to reduce inflammation and prevent the progression of atherosclerosis. NP‐based drug delivery systems are developed to deliver drugs to the brain defying the blood‐brain barrier to treat neurological disorders like the Alzheimer's disease.[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0015), [16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0016), [17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0017)

Despite the numerous advantages of NP‐based smart delivery systems, several challenges are also there to address. For example, the synthesis and characterization of NPs could be complex, and their stability and toxicity need careful evaluation. The targeting and controlled drug release efficiency of NP‐based delivery systems for specific applications also need optimization.[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0018)

4. LIPOSOMAL DRUG DELIVERY
--------------------------

Liposomes are spherical lipid bilayer vesicles that could encapsulate hydrophilic or hydrophobic drugs. They are biocompatible, and biodegradable, and can be functionalized for improved specificity with targeting ligands. Liposomes can be engineered to respond to stimuli, such as altering pH, temperature, and light, to release drugs at the desired site of action.They can also be used to improve drug stability and solubility.[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0019), [20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0020)

On the other hand, polymeric NPs are composed of biodegradable or biocompatible polymers like PLGA and PEG. These can also be functionalized with targeting ligands and respond to pH, temperature, and enzyme stimuli.Polymeric NPs can encapsulate both hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs, making them versatile drug carriers.[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0021) Both liposomes and polymeric NPs have been used in clinical trials for various diseases including cancer, infectious diseases, and inflammatory disorders and have shown astounding results at the preclinical stage.[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0022), [23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0023)

5. MAGNETIC DRUG DELIVERY
-------------------------

Magnetic NPs having the ability to respond to magnetic fields have been explored for smart drug delivery. Such NPs can be functionalised with drugs and targeting ligands and delivered to target sites using an external magnetic field thereby improving drug delivery specificity and efficacy, especially in the hard‐to‐reach areas such as the brain.[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0024) Magnetic NPs can also be used in hyperthermia therapy, where they are heated using an external magnetic field to induce tumor cell death. With promising results in preclinical trials, it holds great promise to improve cancer therapy.[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0025), [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0026)

6. MICELLES FOR SMART DRUG DELIVERY
-----------------------------------

Due to their unique structural and physicochemical properties, micelle NPs can be used as smart drug delivery systems. These are composed of amphiphilic molecules that could self‐assemble in an aqueous solution to form stable colloidal particles with a hydrophobic core and a hydrophilic outer layer. The hydrophobic core could encapsulate hydrophobic drugs, enhance their solubility and stability by protecting them from degrading, while the hydrophilic outer layer could improve the stability and biocompatibility.[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0027), [28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0028)

One primary advantage of micelles smart drug delivery systems is their ability to selectively accumulate in a tumor microenvironment owing to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. Tumors demonstrate leaky and disorganized blood vessels and impaired lymphatic drainage that result in the accumulation of high concentration of NPs in tumors as compared to normal tissues, a EPR phenomenon.[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0029) Micelles can be designed to exploit the EPR effect by incorporating targeting ligands like antibodies or peptides on their surface that would enhance the specificity and efficacy of drug delivery.[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0030), [31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0031), [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0032)

Another advantage of micelles as smart drug delivery systems is their ability to respond to stimuli like the altered pH, temperature, or redox potential. Stimuli‐responsive micelles can be designed to release the drug on the tumors in response to triggers like acidic environment or the presence of certain enzymes, allowing for target‐specific controlled drug release. pH‐responsive micelles release the drug in the acidic tumor microenvironment, reduce systemic toxicity, and improve drug efficacy.[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0033), [34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0034)

Despite these advantages, there are several challenges that need to be addressed. For example, the stability and biocompatibility of micelles, and the efficiency and specificity of drug delivery need to be carefully evaluated.[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0007) Additionally, micelles synthesis and characterization can be complex, and the scalability of the production process needs to be optimized. Nevertheless, the continued advancements in micelle‐based drug delivery systems provide exciting opportunities for improved healthcare outcomes and ultimately achieve personalized medicine goals.[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0035)

7. STIMULI‐RESPONSIVE HYDROGELS‐BASED SMART DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEMS
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Owing to their unique properties of high‐water content, biocompatibility and responsiveness to environmental stimuli, stimuli‐responsive hydrogels have emerged as promising materials to design smart drug delivery systems.[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0036) Hydrogels are 3‐D networks of cross‐linked polymers that can absorb and retain large amount of water while their structural integrity is still retained. The responsiveness of hydrogels to external stimuli like temperature, pH, and ionic strength can be tailored by incorporating stimuli‐responsive polymers like poly‐N‐isopropylacrylamide, poly‐acrylic acid, or poly‐ethylene glycol into the hydrogel matrix.[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0037), [38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0038)

Stimuli‐responsive hydrogels as smart drug delivery are advantageous due to their ability to provide stimuli‐specific controlled release of drugs. Temperature‐responsive designed hydrogels release drugs in response to an altered temperature, like elevated temperature in tumors, allowing for localized sustained drug delivery. pH‐responsive hydrogels could be designed to release drugs in response to the tumourogenic acidic microenvironment that enhance specificity and efficiency of drug delivery.[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0039) Additionally, ionic strength‐responsive designed hydrogels release drugs in response to changes in the ionic strength of the surrounding environment, such as high ionic strength in the gastrointestinal tract, improving the bioavailability and therapeutic effect of the orally‐administered drugs.[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0040)

Another advantage of stimuli‐responsive hydrogels is their ability to be modified with targeting ligands or imaging agents, enabling targeted drug delivery and real‐time monitoring of the drug release. Targeting ligands like antibodies or peptides could conjugate to the surface of hydrogels to enhance the specificity and efficiency of drug delivery to the target site. Imaging agents like fluorescent dyes or magnetic NPs can be incorporated into hydrogels for real‐time in vivo monitoring of drug release and localization.[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0040), [41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0041)

Despite these advantages, there are several challenges to address for stimuli‐responsive hydrogels as smart drug delivery systems. For instance, their stability and biocompatibility, the efficiency and specificity of drug delivery need to be carefully evaluated. Also, the synthesis and characterization of hydrogels and the scalability of the production process can be complex and need consideration. The ongoing advancements in hydrogel‐based drug delivery systems nevertheless provide exciting opportunities and promise a better healthcare system.[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0042)

8. BIOMIMETIC SMART DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEMS
-----------------------------------------

Biomimetic smart drug delivery systems are designed to mimic the structure of biological systems such as cells or tissues and the natural process to improve drug delivery specificity and efficiency.[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040566/#hsr22065-bib-0038) Biomimetic drug delivery systems could incorporate various biological system features, such as cell‐penetrating peptides, extracellular matrix components, or biological signaling molecules, to enhance the targeting and uptake of drugs by the target cells or tissues. Abstract
--------

With the unprecedented progresses of biomedical nanotechnology during the past few decades, conventional drug delivery systems (DDSs) have been involved into smart DDSs with stimuli-responsive characteristics. Benefiting from the response to specific internal or external triggers, those well-defined nanoplatforms can increase the drug targeting efficacy, in the meantime, reduce side effects/toxicities of payloads, which are key factors for improving patient compliance. In academic field, variety of smart DDSs have been abundantly demonstrated for various intriguing systems, such as stimuli-responsive polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, metals/metal oxides, and exosomes _._ However, these nanoplatforms are lack of standardized manufacturing method, toxicity assessment experience, and clear relevance between the pre-clinical and clinical studies, resulting in the huge difficulties to obtain regulatory and ethics approval. Therefore, such relatively complex stimulus-sensitive nano-DDSs are not currently approved for clinical use. In this review, we highlight the recent advances of smart nanoplatforms for targeting drug delivery. Furthermore, the clinical translation obstacles faced by these smart nanoplatforms have been reviewed and discussed. We also present the future directions and perspectives of stimuli-sensitive DDS in clinical applications.

**Keywords:** Controlled release, Smart nanoplatform, Drug delivery system (DDS), Biomaterials, Nanomedicine.

1. Introduction
---------------

To enhance their therapeutic effects and reduce the related side effects, active drug molecules should selectively accumulate in the disease area for a prolonged period with high controllability. Drug delivery refers to the approaches, formulations, technologies, and systems for transporting therapeutics in the body as needed to safely and efficiently achieve their desired therapeutic effects [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B1). Conventional drug delivery systems (DDSs) are often accompanied by systemic side effects that mainly attributable to their nonspecific bio-distribution and uncontrollable drug release characteristics. To overcome these limitations, advanced controlled DDSs have been developed to achieve the release of payloads at the target sites in a spatial controlled manner. In comparison to the conventional DDSs, the smart controlled DDSs can effectively reduce the dosage frequency, while maintaining the drug concentration in targeted organs/tissues for a longer period of time. In this sense, the controlled DDSs provide broad insights and fascinating properties for decreasing drug concentration fluctuation, reducing drug toxicities and improving therapeutic efficacy.

Due to their unique nanoscale properties and specific bio-functions, various nanomaterials provide intriguing benefits and new opportunities for the smart DDSs. For example, nanoparticle-based DDSs can selectively accumulate and specifically bind to the disease target with controlled release behavior. Although recent research and reviews have developed and summarized several novel aspects for nanomaterials that being used as smart drug carriers [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B1)-[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B6), few of them have been finally translated into clinics for real-world applications [7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B7)-[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B9). To our point of view, there are several essential components should be taken into account to ensure clinical potential for future marketing (Figure [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#F1)) [10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B10). On one hand, the design of nanomaterials as drug carriers should address the following key issues: (i) sufficient biocompatibility and biodegradability; (ii) good stability in physiological conditions; and (iii) high drug loading capacity and low toxicity. On the other hand, besides the primary requirement for safety and therapeutic efficacy, industrial scale-up for DDSs is also a prerequisite for this type of novel nanomaterials in clinical applications as shown in Figure [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#F1).

To date, a myriad of materials, such as polymers, lipids and inorganic materials, have been developed and served as drug carriers to control the release behavior of payloads [11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B11)-[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B16), making the drugs “smart”. In this review, we summarize the well-defined smart carriers, including the smart polymer carriers, liposome, organic-inorganic hybrid smart nanoparticles, exosomes, and other nanomaterials, for controlled drug release. Moreover, the clinical application potentialities of controlled drug delivery nanoplatforms, as well as the obstacles faced by those nanoplatforms in clinical translation have been reviewed and discussed.

2. Design rationale of smart drug delivery nanoplatforms
--------------------------------------------------------

It is well known that drugs should ideally be released at the target sites in a controlled manner to enhance their therapeutic efficiency, meanwhile, to reduce the side effects. Inheriting from the controlled release nanoplatforms, the loaded drugs can act “smart”. Since the phase transition of polyacrylamide gels was observed by Tanaka in 1978 [17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B17), the research on phase transition of polymeric gels was greatly boosted. Almost in the same period, thermal-sensitive liposomes was first reported for drug delivery [18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B18). Gradually, the stimuli-responsive biomaterials have been developed and widely used for controlled drug delivery. With the development of nanotechnology and nanomaterials, drugs can also be conjugated with different nanoparticles (see section 3.3). By utilizing the excellent size and surface properties, the nanomaterials are acting as one of the most promising smart DDSs.

In this review, “Smart DDSs” is determined as the process that the drugs are not released before reaching target tissues/ organs (or with extremely slow rate), and only released with proper rates at the sites of action. Although the drug molecules themselves sometimes can be smart components, here we primarily discuss about smart DDSs by means of nanotechnology to carry the drug molecules. The delicate design of smart nanoplatform enables the release of payloads at specific tissues in systemic administration. Currently, the drug-loaded nanoplatform ensures that the drug will not freely extravasate during the blood circulation, but only release at the targets where the nanocarriers accumulate by active or passive targeting strategy. These elaborately designed smart or stimuli-responsive nanoplatforms can respond to endogenous and/ or exogenous stimulus as shown in Figure [2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#F2). The endogenous triggers such as pH variations, hormone level, enzyme concentration, small bio-molecules, glucose or redox gradient [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B19), [20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B20), which are related to the disease pathological characteristics. While the exogenous triggers, including temperature, magnetic field, ultrasound (US), light, electric pulse/ high energy radiation, can also be used to trigger or enhance the drug release at diseased areas. The rationales of different stimuli-responsive nanoplatforms will be briefly discussed as below.

### 2.1 pH-responsive

Among different types of stimuli, pH is one of the most frequently used triggers for drug release [21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B21)-[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B24). The conventional pH-responsive carriers are based on significant variation of pH values in different organs, such as stomach (pH≈ 2) and intestinal tract (pH≈ 7). For example, Eudragit S100 coated citrus pectin nanoparticles (E-CPNs) were prepared for the colon specific targeting of 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) [25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B25). The designed carriers can sensitively differentiate delicate pH changes in specific disease sites, such as inflammatory, ischemic, and tumor tissues, even in different organelles, like endosomes and lysosomes. One typical example is the pH-responsive nanocarriers for solid tumor targeting [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B26). The extracellular pH in normal tissue and blood is usually maintained at around 7.4. However, because of high rate of glycolysis, the mean extracellular pH values in various solid tumors are usually below 7.0 [27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B27), [28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B28). The low pH in the tumor extracellular matrix can be used as a specific stimulus in controlled DDSs. Furthermore, pH difference can also be found in organelles such as in endosomes and lysosomes, in which the pH value is lower than other intracellular organelles with the pH range from 4.5 to 5.5 [21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B21), [28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B28). Thus, the pH variety is the key design rationale for the development of advanced DDSs.

Although the stimulus of pH is widely used in smart DDS, it still needs to be combined with other stimuli, such as temperature, redox, to achieve extremely precise and specific release at the target sites [29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B29)-[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B31).

### 2.2 Redox-responsive

Redox responsive stimuli have gained great attention for disease therapy and widely used in intracellular DDSs [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B19), [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B32). The redox potential in microenvironments is multivariate in different tissues, which can be exploited to design redox-sensitive delivery systems. The design and fabrication of nanoparticles responsive to Glutathione (GSH) can be a promising approach for targeting drug delivery [33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B33). The GSH reduction is a well-known redox system within cancer cells. On one hand, concentrations of GSH in blood and normal extracellular matrices are reported to be 2-20 μM, at the same time GSH levels within cancer cell ranges from 2 to 10 mM, which is 100- to 500- fold higher than the normal ranges [34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B34). Such significant difference in GSH level between cancer and normal cell has made redox responsive delivery systems to be an attractive strategy to design the DDSs for targeting specific tumor intracellular sites. On the other hand, by utilizing the high accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in some disease tissues, ROS-response DDS is also an effective mechanism to finely control the targeted drug release. It has been reported the mucosal ROS concentrations in inflammatory tissues and colon cancer were 10- to 100- fold higher than that of normal tissues [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B19), [35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B35). The exciting specificity and accuracy have been shown by the developed redox stimuli-responsive DDSs, however, it is difficult to achieve the specific redox molecular mechanism based controllability due to the complex biological environment and hetereogeniety of tumor cells.

### 2.3 Enzyme-responsive

Enzymes used as triggers in the design of smart DDSs have been an emerging field in recent years owing to its unique superiorities, such as substrate specificity and high selectivity under mild conditions [36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B36)-[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B39). Since enzymes (such as glycosidases, lipase, phospoholipases or proteases) are related to almost all the biological and metabolic process, they can be exploited to achieve enzyme-mediated drug release by the bio-catalytic action at the cancer or inflammation tissues [38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B38). A major challenge in enzyme-responsive DDSs is to precisely control the initial response time of the systems.

### 2.4 Temperature-responsive

Temperature is one of the most convenient and effective factors to control the drug release compared with other stimuli [40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B40)-[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B42). Normally, pathophysiological conditions, such as inflammation and tumors, have higher temperatures than normal tissues [43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B43). Considering the temperature difference between cancer tissues and normal tissues, functionalized nanoparticles can be triggered to enhance their drug release in tumors [44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B44), [45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B45). Another temperature- responsive strategy is that the tumor site could be heated by external triggers (US, magnetic field, _etc._) to improve the drug release within the tumor vasculature microenvironment [34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B34). In general, thermo-sensitive nanocarriers are designed to retain their payloads around the physiological temperature of 37°C, and release the payloads rapidly when temperature is increased higher than 40-45°C. Towards the thermo-responsive nanoplatforms, the current challenge is to maintain the safety of the platforms without sacrificing their sensitivity to slight temperature changes [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B19).

### 2.5 Light, magnetic, and US responsive

Light-responsive systems represent a way to trigger drug release at the desired target by external light illumination. Photo sensitive carriers can achieve the on-off drug release event because the nanostructure may open or close when stimulated by either a one-time or repeatable light irradiation [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B19). However, considering the limitation of light wavelength for practical therapy, light penetration depth currently restrains the non-invasive applications for deep tissues [46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B46).

Magnetic stimuli may provide a non-invasive approach to temporally and spatially control of the carriers to the targets and release drugs under programmable exposure of external magnetic field [47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B47)-[49](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B49). For example, the most commonly-used core/shell magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) exhibit a variety of unique magnetic properties. The large surface-to-volume ratio of MNPs provides abundant active sites for biomolecule conjugation, thus allowing precise design and engineering in order to gain their intended smart functions by applying a localized external magnetic field, such as long-lasting circulation in the blood stream, target specificity to lesion tissues, and therapeutic delivery. Furthermore, when these nano-scaled MNPs were encapsulated in colloidal carriers, such as micelles, liposomes, or solid nanoparticles [34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B34), [50](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B50)-[53](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B53), the composite structures might become sensitive to an external magnetic field to realize multifunctional formulations for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes [54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B54)-[60](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B60).

Recently, US has been extensively used in clinics for diagnosis and therapy due to its intrinsic tissue penetration and high safety. The development of ultrasonic sensitive nanocarriers for ultrasonography expands US techniques to be a unique and effective method to capture drug carriers and trigger drug release at the desired sites by tuning the US frequency, duty cycles and time of exposure [61](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B61).

### 2.6 Other responsive systems

Apart from the above-mentioned stimuli for smart DDSs, glucose [62](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B62)-[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B64) and electro-responsive systems [65](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B65)-[68](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B68) have also been employed to control the release of payloads within nanocarriers. Moreover, sensitivity to hybrid stimulus can further improve drug delivery accuracy. Dual stimuli-responsive DDSs are most common systems and have been investigated, such as thermo- and pH- responsive systems [69](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B69), [70](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B70), thermo- and light- responsive systems [71](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B71), [72](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B72), redox- and pH- -responsive systems [30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B30), [31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B31), ultrasonic and magnetic responsive systems [73](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B73)-[77](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B77).

In order to realize the smart characteristic of DDSs, a wide range of stimuli that are able to trigger the drug release at target place and expected time has been assembled in various nano-architectures. To ascertain the viability of these strategies, evidence for regulation of the response to each stimulus would be needed both _in vitro_ and _in vivo_. In this review, we mainly discuss smart nanoplatforms with the most promising clinical potential in the application of stimuli-responsive DDSs. Smart DDSs for controlled drug release (_e.g._, polymers, liposome, organic-inorganic hybrid biomaterials and exosomes) are discussed. Although smart nanoplatforms can be widely used in several diseased, such as neoplastic diseases, diabetes, infections, cardiovascular diseases and inflammatory diseases, this review is mainly focused on carcinoma diseases for the future clinical translation of smart DDSs.

3. Smart nanoscale DDS
----------------------

### 3.1 Polymeric nanoparticles

Polymers with the ability to respond to external physical or chemical stimulus are defined as smart or stimuli-responsive polymers. Half century ago, smart polymers have been utilized to control the release of biologically active cargos, which played a very important role in the research and development of nanomedicines. As introduced in previous section, various external stimuli have been reported in smart polymeric systems, including US, pH, and magnetic fields [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B1), [7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B7), [16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B16). The physical/ chemical properties of polymers can smartly transform in responding to those stimuli. The drug release rate can be controlled by the intensity of applied stimuli to the fabricated carriers.

These smart polymer materials can be divided into two categories: single stimulus and dual-/ multi-stimuli responsive polymers [29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B29). Single stimulus that induces protonation, hydrolytic cleavage or (supra) molecular conformational change in the polymer nanomaterials, which can be further categorized as exogenous and/or endogenous stimulus [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B19). The exogenous stimuli such as temperature, magnetic field, ionic strength, US intensity or electric pulses can trigger conformation changes due to on-off polymer chains [78](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B78). Factors like pH, enzyme concentration, hormone level, redox gradient, and small bio-molecules are summarized as endogenous stimuli [79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B79). Figure [3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#F3) shows the process of using dual and multi-stimuli smart polymeric nanomaterials for controlling the drug release in solid tumors [29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B29). Among a variety of stimuli that have been utilized, pH, redox, enzymes, light, and temperature have recently emerged as promising triggering motif for the design of smart polymeric DDSs [20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B20), [29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B29), [79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4924501/#B79).

One is that the nanocarriers can be activated to release cargos by pH variety when arriving at slightly acidic tumor sites. ABSTRACT Traditional drug delivery systems (DDSs) often cause side effects, prompting the need for smarter alternatives. Intelligent or smart drug delivery systems offer targeted, on-demand medication release, improving therapeutic outcomes while reducing side effects and dosage frequency. These systems use stimuli-responsive materials like polymers, lipids, or inorganic substances to detect changes in the body and release drugs accordingly. Benefits include maintaining consistent plasma drug levels, localized drug delivery, preserving unstable drugs, enhancing stability, and improving patient compliance.(1) To improve therapeutic effects and reduce side effects, active drug molecules should selectively accumulate in diseased areas for extended periods with precise control. Drug delivery involves methods and technologies that transport therapeutics safely and effectively within the body. Traditional drug delivery systems often cause systemic side effects due to nonspecific distribution and uncontrolled release. To address this, advanced controlled drug delivery systems (DDSs) have been developed, enabling targeted and sustained release at specific sites. These systems reduce dosage frequency, maintain consistent drug levels, minimize toxicity, and enhance therapeutic outcomes. I. INTRODUCTION Nanomaterials, due to their nanoscale properties and specific bio-functions, offer promising advantages for smart drug delivery systems (DDSs). Nanoparticle-based DDSs can selectively target diseased areas and control drug release. Despite significant research advancements, few have reached clinical application due to challenges in design and scalability [3]. Key considerations for clinical translation include biocompatibility, biodegradability, physiological stability, high drug loading capacity, and low toxicity. Additionally, large-scale production remains essential. Various materials like polymers, lipids, and inorganics have been utilized to make DDSs more efficient and “smart” [4,5]. These systems enhance targeted delivery, maximizing efficacy and minimizing side effects through various administration routes beyond oral, such as nasal, transdermal, and subcutaneous. DDSs improve systemic circulation and drug control, vital for effective treatment. The concept of controlled release, first approved in the 1950s, has since gained attention for offering predictable, long-lasting drug delivery unaffected by physiological changes. This approach enhances solubility, targeting, pharmacokinetics, and patient compliance while reducing toxicity [6]. ecently, novel drug delivery systems (NDDS) have been developed to offer more controlled, convenient, and targeted drug delivery. The release rate and mechanism of these systems vary depending on their physical, chemical, and morphological characteristics. Major release mechanisms include diffusion, chemical or solvent reactions, and stimuli-responsive control. One widely studied approach is the Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect, a passive targeting mechanism where nanoparticles exploit the porous vasculature of tumors to accumulate at the disease site. While effective, EPR lacks selectivity and can increase toxicity [6]. Active targeting addresses this by using ligands or molecules that bind specifically to target cells, improving selectivity and reducing side effects. However, challenges such as immunogenicity, ligand specificity, and lysosomal degradation after macrophage uptake remain. Additionally, stimuli-responsive systems that react to factors like pH, temperature, ultrasound, and magnetic or electric fields are being explored to further enhance targeted delivery. II. SMART DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEMS A Smart Drug Delivery System (SDDS) is an advanced form of targeted drug delivery that enhances therapeutic efficacy and minimizes side effects by ensuring selective accumulation of active drug molecules at the disease site for a prolonged duration with high controllability [8]. These systems are designed to cross biological membranes and release accurate drug dosages to the target area. Drug delivery systems (DDS), which range from implantable devices to single polymer chains, must be biocompatible with both the body and the drug. They alter the pharmacokinetics and biodistribution of drugs, determining the concentration of the drug in various organs over time. Biocompatibility is evaluated via in vitro tests—such as cell viability, ROS, and immune activation—and in vivo assessments including inflammation, immune cell infiltration, and impacts on organs. Design also plays a key role, with device geometry and material flexibility tailored to application sites like the eye, skin, mouth, or vagina. Optical transparency is essential for ocular devices, while mechanical flexibility and adhesion are critical for skin-contact devices. Advances in self-healing, stretchable biomaterials (e.g., PVA-tannic acid) support durability and drug delivery. Effective WDs should improve patient compliance, offer long-acting therapy, and align with specific design needs, such as drug-eluting contact lenses [12–14]. 3.1 Enfuse -A Wearable Delivery system EnFuse is an innovative wearable drug delivery system designed to facilitate the self-administration of large volumes of small-molecule and biologic therapeutics—up to 50 mL—by patients in the comfort of their own homes [15]. MN arrays, consisting of micro-sized needles ranging from 25 to 2000 μm in height, penetrate the stratum corneum to deliver drugs in a minimally invasive, painless manner [20]. These systems have been explored for a variety of applications including drug and vaccine delivery, cosmetic treatments, and disease diagnostics. Compared to traditional delivery routes—oral, parenteral, ophthalmic, and transdermal—each has distinct advantages and limitations. Oral delivery, while convenient and cost-effective, often suffers from issues like drug degradation, first-pass metabolism, and inconsistent absorption. The parenteral route provides rapid and accurate delivery but is invasive and requires medical expertise. Transdermal delivery avoids first-pass metabolism and enhances patient compliance, but its effectiveness is limited by the skin’s barrier, especially the stratum corneum. MNs help overcome this limitation by enhancing drug transport across the skin without pain, enabling efficient and targeted delivery even for drugs that traditionally struggle with skin permeability [20]. Microneedles (MNs) can be classified based on their drug delivery mechanisms into several types, each designed to optimize delivery efficiency and patient comfort. Solid MNs are made from a single material and contain no drug; they are primarily used to create micro-channels in the skin to enhance drug permeation from topical formulations applied afterward. Hollow MNs, resembling miniaturized hypodermic needles, contain a central bore that allows the infusion of liquid drugs directly into the skin. Coated MNs are solid needles with a drug formulation layered on their surface; upon insertion, the coating dissolves into the skin while the needle is removed. Dissolvable or biodegradable MNs are fabricated from water-soluble or biodegradable polymers or sugars that encapsulate the drug. These dissolve or degrade upon insertion, releasing the drug payload and leaving no harmful residue. Hydrogel-forming MNs, a subtype of biodegradable MNs, swell upon absorbing interstitial fluid to enable sustained drug release through the channels they create. Rapidly separating MNs consist of a drug-loaded, water-soluble microneedle array mounted on an insoluble backing. Upon insertion, the drug-containing portion dissolves quickly, and the backing is removed, simplifying application and improving usability. These diverse types of MNs expand the potential for targeted, pain-free, and efficient transdermal drug delivery across various therapeutic applications. CHALLENGES IN WEARABLE DEVICES Wearable drug delivery devices (WDDs) offer innovative solutions for transdermal drug administration but face several technical challenges. A primary limitation is their suitability predominantly for small-molecule, lipophilic drugs that can efficiently penetrate the skin barrier and are effective at low concentrations. This restricts their application for macromolecular drugs, such as nucleic acids and proteins, and for treatments requiring high-dose administration, like in cancer or infectious diseases. Additionally, the integration of WDDs with biological surfaces is complicated by the presence of body fluids (e.g., sweat, saliva, vaginal fluids), which can affect device adhesion and performance. Emerging strategies, including swellable microneedle patches, gecko-inspired adhesives, and pressure-sensitive adhesives, are being explored to enhance device adherence, though their long-term safety remains under investigation. Moreover, the limited drug payload capacity of WDDs poses challenges for high-dose therapies, and increasing device size to accommodate more drug can lead to patient discomfort and potential detachment due to body movements. Repeated application of microneedle patches to maintain dosing can also cause skin irritation and increase infection risk. Therefore, future research should focus on improving drug loading capacities, ensuring stable integration with biological surfaces, and assessing the long-term safety of new adhesive technologies to expand the clinical applicability of WDDs. V. STIMULI RESPONSIVE DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEM Nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems have emerged as a promising area of research in pharmaceutical and biomedical sciences, particularly for enhancing the efficacy of drugs such as anticancer, antiviral, and antimicrobial agents. These systems offer advantages in targeted drug delivery and controlled release, minimizing side effects and improving therapeutic outcomes. Nanoparticles, including liposomes, polymeric nanoparticles, micelles, protein-based nanoparticles, and inorganic nanoparticles, are commonly employed in drug delivery applications. The targeting ability of nanoparticles is primarily achieved through two mechanisms: passive and active targeting. Passive targeting relies on the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, where nanoparticles accumulate in tumor tissues due to their abnormal vasculature. Active targeting involves the functionalization of nanoparticles with ligands that specifically bind to receptors overexpressed on diseased cells, facilitating targeted drug delivery. While passive targeting is widely utilized in marketed nanoparticle drug delivery systems, active targeting offers the potential for more precise delivery. Stimuli-responsive drug delivery systems (DDS) have been developed to further enhance the specificity and control of drug release. These systems can be classified based on the nature of the stimulus: endogenous or exogenous. Endogenous Stimuli-Responsive DDS: Endogenous stimuli-responsive DDS utilize internal biochemical and chemical cues to trigger drug release. These cues include:  Redox-responsive systems: Sensitive to changes in the redox environment, often utilizing the higher glutathione (GSH) concentrations found in tumor cells.  Enzyme-responsive systems: Designed to release drugs upon interaction with specific enzymes, such as proteases or hyaluronidases, which are overexpressed in certain disease states.  pH-responsive systems: Capitalize on the acidic microenvironments of tumors or inflamed tissues to trigger drug release.  Ionic microenvironment-responsive systems: Responsive to changes in ionic strength or composition, which can influence drug release profiles. These systems are advantageous for targeted therapy, as they respond to the unique biochemical conditions present in diseased tissues. Exogenous Stimuli-Responsive DDS: Exogenous stimuli-responsive DDS are controlled by external factors, allowing for precise modulation of drug release. Common external stimuli include: e-ISSN: 2582-5208 International Research Journal of Modernization in Engineering Technology and Science ( Peer-Reviewed, Open Access, Fully Refereed International Journal ) Volume:07/Issue:04/April-2025 Impact Factor- 8.187 www.irjmets.com www.irjmets.com @International Research Journal of Modernization in Engineering, Technology and Science [10587]  Temperature: Thermo-responsive polymers undergo phase transitions at specific temperatures, facilitating drug release.  Magnetic fields: Magnetic nanoparticles can be manipulated using external magnetic fields to control drug delivery.  Light: Photo-responsive systems release drugs upon exposure to specific wavelengths of light.  Electrical fields: Electro-responsive systems utilize electric fields to trigger drug release.  Ultrasound: Ultrasound waves can be used to induce drug release from certain DDS. These systems offer the advantage of external control, enabling on-demand drug release and reducing inter-patient variability. In summary, nanoparticle-based stimuli-responsive DDS represent a significant advancement in drug delivery technologies. By leveraging both endogenous and exogenous stimuli, these systems can provide targeted, controlled, and efficient drug release, improving therapeutic outcomes and minimizing side effects. VI. FUTURE PROSPECTS The future of smart drug delivery systems (DDSs) is poised to transform personalized medicine by offering targeted, controlled, and efficient therapeutic interventions. These advanced systems, including stimuli-responsive nanocarriers, aim to address the limitations of conventional drug delivery methods, such as poor bioavailability and off-target effects. By responding to specific internal or external stimuli—such as pH, temperature, or light—smart DDSs can release therapeutic agents precisely at the site of action, thereby enhancing treatment efficacy and minimizing side effects . Despite their promise, the clinical translation of smart DDSs faces several challenges. One significant hurdle is the variability between animal models and human physiology, which complicates the extrapolation of preclinical results to human therapies. Additionally, the manufacturing processes for these systems are often complex and lack standardization, leading to issues with reproducibility and quality control. The incorporation of novel materials, such as biodegradable polymers and lipids, raises safety concerns regarding cytotoxicity and immunogenicity, necessitating thorough evaluation before clinical application . Furthermore, assessing the bioavailability of drugs delivered via smart DDSs presents unique challenges. Traditional methods may not adequately measure how effectively these systems release and distribute drugs within the body. Developing specialized analytical techniques is essential to accurately evaluate the performance of these advanced delivery systems. Cost-effectiveness remains a critical consideration for the widespread adoption of smart DDSs. The production of these sophisticated systems can be expensive, and without clear evidence of their superiority over existing therapies, healthcare providers may be hesitant to incorporate them into clinical practice. Therefore, demonstrating both the clinical benefits and economic viability of smart DDSs is crucial for their integration into mainstream medicine . In summary, while smart drug delivery systems hold significant potential to revolutionize personalized medicine, addressing the challenges of clinical translation, manufacturing standardization, safety evaluation, bioavailability assessment, and cost-effectiveness is essential for their successful implementation. VII. CONCLUSION The pharmaceutical industry is evolving towards more specialized, personalized treatments, with smart drug delivery systems (DDSs) such as nanocarriers and theragnostic systems showing great potential. These systems offer targeted, controlled drug delivery, addressing the limitations of current approaches and improving efficacy, especially for complex diseases like cancer. However, their clinical adoption is slow due to challenges in quality control, reproducibility, safety concerns, and the high cost of development.
